scenario: i'm doing okay aside from this left shoulder pain that i've been having for a few weeks now
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hey betty how're you"] i'm doing okay aside from this left shoulder pain that i've been having for a few weeks now

[Clinician asked: "wow well i'm sorry to hear that do you remember what you were doing when the pain started"] i you know i ca n't think about anything specifically but i am very active and i also have been renovating my basement so i have been doing a lot with that

[Clinician asked: "wow okay and do you know if there was any direct or associated trauma that you can think of over the past three weeks like did you fall or hit your shoulder or anything"] no no trauma that i can think of at all
[Clinician asked: "okay"] i do n't remember hitting it either
[Clinician asked: "okay now have you ever had pain in that shoulder before"] you know i'm active and will have aches and pains here and there but nothing that some tylenol ca n't take care of
[Clinician asked: "okay and and it's isolated to your left shoulder"] it is

[Clinician asked: "okay are you able to move that left arm for me"] you know anytime i try reaching for something or lifting anything i have a lot of pain and i do n't even try to raise my arm over my head because that's it it really hurts

[Clinician asked: "okay so now i wan na talk a little bit more about that pain are you having pain all of the time or does it wax and wane does it come and go"] it's all the time and if there is any pressure like when i try to sleep at night i ca n't even lay i'm a side sleeper and it i really gives me a hard time because i'm so uncomfortable so the pain is always there
[Clinician asked: "okay so you ca n't lay on your left side at all"] i ca n't
[Clinician asked: "alright now do you have that pain when you lay on your back as well or just on your left side"] i mean it's always there but when i sleep directly on it the pain goes up

[Clinician asked: "okay now let's talk just briefly about your pain so if you have n't taken anything at all or have you been taking anything at all let's start with that have you been taking anything for the pain"] yeah i've been taking tylenol and i'll take two extra strengths tylenol every six to eight hours
[Clinician asked: "okay"] and it does help take the edge off
[Clinician asked: "okay that's good now without any tylenol what's your pain level"] without any tylenol i would say my pain is like a three
[Clinician asked: "okay and then with tylenol with that"] i'm sorry i keep doing that without any tylenol my pain is like a seven and with tylenol it goes down to a two or three
[Clinician asked: "okay that's good so the tylenol really does help you that that's a good thing"] yeah

[Clinician asked: "so you talked about renovating your basement did you guys just move in or is it just time for a little house facelift"] yeah it just time we've been living in this house for some time now and you know with the pandemic we've been home and so decided to do some work
[Clinician asked: "nice have you been into the new home depot that they just opened down the street here"] no

[Clinician asked: "yeah it's nice my wife and i were there this weekend and they just opened two weeks ago so everything's fully stocked and if you guys are doing some renovations you might run down there and you know save big money at home depot"] i definitely will check that out
[Clinician asked: "so are you experiencing any numbness or tingling in your arm or hand"] no

[Clinician asked: "okay so if you do n't mind i'd like to go ahead and do a quick physical exam i've reviewed your vitals everything looks good there and i'm gon na do a focused exam of your left shoulder now you have limited active and passive range of motion there is some tenderness of the greater tuberosity of the "] yeah so i'm really active so do you think that this pain will ever go away

[Clinician asked: "i do i do believe that that pain is gon na go away many of my patients are successful with pt so we are gon na start with that take a conservative approach and see how you do"] okay alright
[Clinician asked: "alright so i'm gon na have the nurse come in and get you some paperwork and i'll see you in about two about two weeks"] alright thank you
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Left shoulder pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Betty Rogers is a pleasant 50-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of left shoulder pain.

For the past few weeks, the patient has been experiencing constant pain isolated to the left shoulder. She denies any known injury or trauma; however, she is very active and is currently renovating her basement. She denies any associated numbness or tingling as well. While movements such as reaching and lifting exacerbate her pain, full flexion of the shoulder is the most severe. Her pain is also exacerbated if she lays on her shoulder, which subsequently causes sleep disturbance due to her being uncomfortable. She typically takes Tylenol Extra Strength, 2 tablets every 6 to 8 hours, for aches and pains associated with her active lifestyle, and doing so reduces her shoulder pain to 2-3/10 on the pain scale. Without medication, she rates her pain as 7/10.

MEDICAL HISTORY

The patient denies any previous trauma or injury to left shoulder.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient reports that she has a very active lifestyle. She has also been renovating her basement recently.

MEDICATIONS

The patient reports that she has been taking Tylenol Extra Strength, 2 tablets every 6 to 8 hours.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports left shoulder pain.
Neurological: Denies left shoulder numbness or tingling.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Capillary refill is brisk at less than 3 seconds.
NEURO: Motor and sensation in the left shoulder are intact to light touch.
MSK: Examination of the left shoulder: Limited active and passive range of motion. Tenderness over the greater tuberosity of the humerus. No tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints. Good hand grip bilaterally.

RESULTS

X-ray imaging of the left shoulder was performed and reviewed in office today. These reveal no fracture or bony abnormality.

ASSESSMENT

Left shoulder pain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I believe this is most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy. We discussed the nature of this condition in detail, and I have ordered an MRI for further evaluation. I'm going to put in a referral for 6 to 8 weeks of physical therapy, during which time she should continue with Tylenol as-needed. If her symptoms do not improve, we can consider administering a steroid injection.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up in 2 weeks.

naive.response_text: I understand how frustrating and limiting this shoulder pain must be, especially since it's affecting your sleep and daily activities. It’s great that you’re being proactive with the physical therapy, as it’s a proven approach to help many people recover from shoulder issues. The exercises and stretches will likely help improve your range of motion and reduce the pain over time. Remember, consistency is key, so try to stick with the PT routine as recommended. If you have any questions or concerns along the way, don’t hesitate to reach out. We’ll keep an eye on your progress and adjust the plan if needed. You’re taking the right steps, and with time, I’m confident you’ll feel better.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns, Betty. It makes sense that you're feeling worried about your shoulder pain, especially since it's been persistent and affecting your daily activities and sleep. I can understand how frustrating it must be, given how active you are and all the physical work you've been doing with the renovation.

We'll start with a conservative approach, focusing on physical therapy to help alleviate the pain and improve your range of motion. It's good that the Tylenol is helping, but we should also consider other strategies to manage the discomfort. If needed, we might explore imaging to better understand the cause, but for now, let's see how physical therapy progresses.

In two weeks, we'll check in on your progress and adjust the plan as needed. Before you leave, could you summarize what we've discussed so far? That way, I can ensure you're clear on the next steps and any recommendations.

framework_only.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns about your left shoulder pain. It sounds like this has been affecting your daily activities and sleep, which I can understand is frustrating.

We'll schedule a follow-up visit in two weeks to monitor your progress and discuss any further treatment options based on how you're feeling.

In the meantime, continue taking the recommended Tylenol as directed and keep track of your pain levels and any changes in your symptoms.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in two weeks. In the meantime, if your pain worsens or you experience any new symptoms like numbness or tingling, please call us right away.

full.response_text:
I can hear how much this has been worrying you — you asked, "Do you think that this pain will ever go away?" That's a real concern, and I want to make sure we address it.

The pain you're experiencing in your left shoulder, which has been constant and especially worse at night, is something we need to explore further. Research shows that while opioids can sometimes help with pain, they aren't always more effective than simpler treatments like acetaminophen or ibuprofen in the short term.

It's understandable to worry about whether this pain will go away. Studies suggest that communication about concerns like these can be helpful in primary care.

We'll start with some tests to better understand what's causing the pain. This includes blood work and imaging if needed.

We'll also schedule a follow-up visit in about 4 weeks to review the results together and discuss the next steps.

In the meantime, if the pain gets worse or you experience any new symptoms, please call us right away.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in 4 weeks. In the meantime, if the pain becomes unbearable or you notice any new symptoms like swelling or weakness in the shoulder, please call us immediately.
